title: Facilities work together to protect patients
section: Clinical Syndromes Health Care Associated Infections

items.subtopic: Facilities work together to protect patients. | ANTIBIOTIC-RESISTANT BACTERIA
items.title: Nosocomial Legionnaires Disease: Detection and Prevention Strategies
items.content: In the event of a single case of nosocomial Legionnaires disease, a comprehensive investigation is warranted. Facilities without prior outbreaks may not require routine water testing for L. pneumophila if effective clinical surveillance is in place. However, institutions with previous cases should integrate regular water testing into their management plans to prevent further occurrences.
keyPoints: Detection of a single nosocomial Legionnaires disease case prompts investigation.
Routine hospital water testing for L. pneumophila not necessary without outbreak history if robust clinical surveillance is present.
Institutions with past cases should conduct regular water testing as part of their management plan.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · Facilities work together to protect patients. | ANTIBIOTIC-RESISTANT BACTERIA

items.subtopic: Facilities work together to protect patients. | ANTIBIOTIC-RESISTANT BACTERIA
items.title: Preventing Transmission of Multidrug-Resistant Organisms in Healthcare Settings
items.content: Healthcare facilities must implement measures to prevent the spread of multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs). Key strategies include meticulous environmental disinfection, coordination during patient transfers, and addressing risk factors such as extended hospital stays and antibiotic exposure. Specific interventions like installing toilet lids and angling sink faucets can reduce transmission risks from contaminated water sources.
keyPoints: 1.27 million deaths globally attributed to antibiotic-resistant infections in 2019.
MRSA and ESBL infections are common MDROs in U.S. hospitals.
Risk factors for MDRO acquisition include healthcare exposure, extended stays, comorbid conditions, device use, and community determinants like unstable housing.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · Facilities work together to protect patients. | ANTIBIOTIC-RESISTANT BACTERIA

items.subtopic: METHICILLIN-RESISTANT STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS | VANCOMYCIN-RESISTANT ENTEROCOCCUS (VRE)
items.title: MRSA Prevention in High-Risk Settings
items.content: In high-risk settings such as ICUs, universal decolonization is more effective than screening and contact precautions alone for reducing MRSA acquisition. This includes the use of chlorhexidine bathing and 5-day nasal application of mupirocin.
keyPoints: Up to a third of MRSA-colonized individuals develop infection; risk in pediatric ICU can be as high as 47%.
Universal decolonization reduces MRSA acquisition more effectively than screening and contact precautions alone.
Post-hospital discharge measures reduce short-term MRSA infection risk by 30%.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · METHICILLIN-RESISTANT STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS | VANCOMYCIN-RESISTANT ENTEROCOCCUS (VRE)

items.subtopic: METHICILLIN-RESISTANT STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS | VANCOMYCIN-RESISTANT ENTEROCOCCUS (VRE)
items.title: Effective Strategies Against VRE Spread
items.content: In hyperendemic settings, active surveillance and contact precautions offer minimal benefit for controlling VRE. However, robust antibiotic stewardship and chlorhexidine baths can reduce the impact by making colonized patients less infectious.
keyPoints: VRE emerged in the U.S. in 1987; early screening controlled its spread when prevalence was low.
Antibiotic exposure to vancomycin, third-generation cephalosporins, and anti-anaerobic agents are risk drivers for VRE acquisition.
Robust antibiotic stewardship and chlorhexidine baths reduce VRE impact by making colonized patients less infectious.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · METHICILLIN-RESISTANT STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS | VANCOMYCIN-RESISTANT ENTEROCOCCUS (VRE)

items.subtopic: MULTIDRUG-RESISTANT GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | DIAGNOSTIC STEWARDSHIP
items.title: Understanding Multidrug Resistance Definitions and Impact on Patient Outcomes
items.content: Multidrug-resistant gram-negative bacteria pose significant challenges in healthcare due to their resistance mechanisms. These organisms are defined by their nonsusceptibility to multiple classes of antibiotics, impacting patient survival negatively compared to susceptible infections.
keyPoints: Multidrug resistance is defined as nonsusceptibility to at least one agent from three distinct antibacterial drug classes.
Difficult-to-treat resistance involves resistance to all first-line antibacterial agents.
Extensively drug-resistant organisms are nonsusceptible to two or fewer antimicrobials in all categories.
Pan-drug resistance means noneffectiveness of all antimicrobial agents.
Decreased survival is noted with infections from multidrug-resistant gram-negative bacteria compared to susceptible ones.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · MULTIDRUG-RESISTANT GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | DIAGNOSTIC STEWARDSHIP

items.subtopic: MULTIDRUG-RESISTANT GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | DIAGNOSTIC STEWARDSHIP
items.title: The Role of Antimicrobial Stewardship in Preventing Multidrug Resistance Spread
items.content: Antimicrobial stewardship is crucial in preventing the emergence and spread of multidrug-resistant gram-negative bacteria. It involves targeted screening, contact precautions, and addressing risk factors like antimicrobial overuse.
keyPoints: Aggressive control measures are essential for prevention.
Antimicrobial stewardship helps prevent the emergence and spread of resistance.
Targeted screening and contact precautions are vital for outbreak management.
Universal gown and glove use did not reduce acquisition rates.
40–60% of testing in hospitalized patients may be medically unnecessary, leading to antibiotic overuse.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · MULTIDRUG-RESISTANT GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | DIAGNOSTIC STEWARDSHIP

items.subtopic: VACCINATION OF HEALTH CARE PERSONNEL | BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE
items.title: Importance of Vaccination for Health Care Personnel
items.content: Health care personnel are at increased risk for acquiring and transmitting vaccine-preventable illnesses. Vaccinations aim to protect personal health, reduce occupational transmission risks, and safeguard vulnerable patients.
keyPoints: Vaccines available for respiratory viruses (influenza, COVID-19), measles, mumps, varicella, pertussis, hepatitis B, meningococcus.
Higher vaccination uptake correlates with lower risk of illness among personnel and nosocomial infection in patients.
Hepatitis B surface antibody testing is recommended 1–2 months post-vaccination; reimmunization if <10 mIU/mL.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · VACCINATION OF HEALTH CARE PERSONNEL | BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE

items.subtopic: VACCINATION OF HEALTH CARE PERSONNEL | BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE
items.title: Management of Hepatitis B Exposure in Health Care Settings
items.content: Health care personnel exposed to hepatitis B should be tested for antibodies post-vaccination. Non-responders are considered susceptible and require specific management if exposed.
keyPoints: Hepatitis B surface antibody test recommended 1–2 months after the last vaccine dose.
Reimmunization is advised if antibody levels are <10 mIU/mL.
Non-responders to repeat vaccination series should be managed as susceptible in case of exposure.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · VACCINATION OF HEALTH CARE PERSONNEL | BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE

items.subtopic: BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE
items.title: Protocols for Bloodborne Pathogen Exposure Management
items.content: Exposure management involves risk evaluation, baseline testing, and timely administration of prophylaxis. Hepatitis B immune globulin is effective within 7 days post-exposure; HIV PEP should start within 72 hours.
keyPoints: Hepatitis B immune globulin ideally given within 24 hours, up to 7 days post-exposure.
HIV postexposure prophylaxis (PEP) must be initiated within 72 hours of exposure.
Serial testing for HIV and hepatitis at 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months if immune or immunized.
items.reference: Facilities work together to protect patients · VACCINATION OF HEALTH CARE PERSONNEL | BLOODBORNE PATHOGEN EXPOSURE

items.question: What is the recommended action in facilities with no history of nosocomial Legionnaires disease upon detection of a single case?
options: Conduct routine water testing for L. pneumophila.
Implement robust clinical surveillance without routine water testing.
Ignore the case if there is no outbreak history.
Transfer all patients to another facility.
items.explanation: Routine hospital water testing for L. pneumophila is not strictly necessary if robust clinical surveillance is in place.

items.explanation: Installing lids on toilets and angling sink faucets can reduce splashback, reducing transmission risk.
